elements.sent-2411-001: Since generative artificial intelligence (AI) tools such as OpenAI's ChatGPT became widely available, researchers have used them in the writing process.
elements.sent-2411-002: The consensus of the academic publishing community is that such usage must be declared in the published article.
elements.sent-2411-003: Academ-AI documents examples of suspected undeclared AI usage in the academic literature, discernible primarily due to the appearance in research papers of idiosyncratic verbiage characteristic of large language model (LLM)--based chatbots.
elements.sent-2411-004: This analysis of the first 500 examples collected reveals that the problem is widespread, penetrating the journals and conference proceedings of highly respected publishers.
elements.sent-2411-005: Undeclared AI seems to appear in journals with higher citation metrics and higher article processing charges (APCs), precisely those outlets that should theoretically have the resources and expertise to avoid such oversights.
elements.sent-2411-006: An extremely small minority of cases are corrected post publication, and the corrections are often insufficient to rectify the problem.
elements.sent-2411-007: The 500 examples analyzed here likely represent a small fraction of the undeclared AI present in the academic literature, much of which may be undetectable.
elements.sent-2411-008: Publishers must enforce their policies against undeclared AI usage in cases that are detectable; this is the best defense currently available to the academic publishing community against the proliferation of undisclosed AI.
elements.sent-2411-009: Artificial intelligence, large language models, chatbots, ChatGPT, generative pre-trained transformer, research integrity, publishing, peer review.
elements.sent-2411-010: Shortly after the public release of OpenAI's ChatGPT in November 2022, researchers began incorporating its use into their workflows.
elements.sent-2411-011: The use of ChatGPT or similar tools based on large language models (LLMs) to generate text for inclusion in research articles was immediately controversial.
elements.sent-2411-012: A consensus rapidly emerged in the academic publishing community on two points:.
elements.sent-2411-013: No artificial intelligence (AI) system may be listed as an author.
elements.sent-2411-014: Any author who uses AI in the writing process must declare that they have done so in the published article.
elements.sent-2411-015: These principles have declared by a variety of academic publishing organizations, including the Committee on Publication Ethics (COPE), the Council of Science Editors (CSE), the International Committee of Medical Journal Editors (ICMJE), the World Association of Medical Editors (WAME), the Institute of Electrical and Electronics Engineers (IEEE), the Institute of Physics (IOP), the Society of Photo-Optical Instrumentation Engineers (SPIE), Elsevier, Frontiers, the Multidisciplinary Digital Publishing Institute (MDPI), the Public Library of Science (PLoS), Sage, Science, Springer, Taylor \& Francis, and Wiley.\footnote{Specifically, all of the listed organizations have declared point \ref{enum-declare , and all but the IEEE and PLoS have declared point \ref{enum-author .}}}
elements.sent-2411-016: The relevant text from these policies is compiled in Appendix \ref{app-policy .}
elements.sent-2411-017: The justification for point \ref{enum-author is that LLMs cannot be held accountable for their statements, but human authors can.}
elements.sent-2411-018: Authorship implies the assumption of responsibility for the contents of a manuscript; since an LLM cannot assume this responsibility, it cannot be an author.
elements.sent-2411-019: Responsibility instead lies with the author who chooses to use an LLM to generate text and incorporate that text into their own work.
elements.sent-2411-020: It is also the author's responsibility to validate such text:.
elements.sent-2411-021: Researchers who use these NLP {[ natural language processing{] systems to write text for their manuscripts must therefore check the text for factual and citation accuracy; bias; mathematical, logical, and commonsense reasoning; relevance; and originality.. \signed{\autocite{hosseini2023.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-022: Point \ref{enum-declare ensures that the implementation of point \ref{enum-author does not compromise transparency.}}
elements.sent-2411-023: LLMs should not be listed as authors, but it is still important for readers to know that they are reading AI-generated text:.
elements.sent-2411-024: Because NLP systems may be used in ways that may not be obvious to the reader, researchers should disclose their use of such systems and indicate which parts of the text were written or co-written by an NLP system.. \signed{\autocite{hosseini2023.}}
elements.sent-2411-025: These are more than academic principles, beyond responsibility or transparency for their own sake.
elements.sent-2411-026: LLMs have a well-documented tendency to ``make up information'', write ``plausible-sounding but incorrect or nonsensical answers'' \autocite{openai2022 , or ``confidently state information that isn't correct'' \autocite{weston2021 .}}
elements.sent-2411-027: This phenomenon is known as ``confabulation,'' ``hallucination,'' or ``fabrication'' and is one of twelve risks novel to or exacerbated by the use of generative AI identified by the United States \textcite[][NIST]{nist2024 .}
elements.sent-2411-028: Examples of the real-world impact of confabulation include several legal cases, in which attorneys cited non-existent case law to support their arguments\footnote{Mata v.}
elements.sent-2411-032: As the NIST emphasizes, confabulation is a consequence of training generative AI models to optimize the next word prediction objective.
elements.sent-2411-033: LLMs such as the generated pretrained transformer (GPT) architecture of ChatGPT (OpenAI) are programmed to predict the next token (part of a word) in a text string.
elements.sent-2411-034: This is, in the words of OpenAI researchers, ``only a proxy for what these models want to do'' \autocite{ouyang2022 .}
elements.sent-2411-035: They are not sentient; they do not ``know'' information or ``understand'' the instructions they are given; such verbiage is inappropriately anthropomorphic, although its use is difficult to avoid entirely.
elements.sent-2411-036: As long as we use LLMs trained to optimize the next word prediction objective, confabulation will occur.
elements.sent-2411-037: It is imperative, therefore, that authors scrutinize the outputs of AI systems to ensure that they are accurate before including them in research manuscripts.
elements.sent-2411-038: Readers must be informed whenever AI is used to generate text so that they may apply their own scrutiny.
elements.sent-2411-039: These principles, while generally agreed upon by the academic publishing community, have not consistently been upheld.
elements.sent-2411-040: Many journals and other academic publications, including those with explicit policies against undeclared generative AI use, have published articles containing what appears to be AI-generated text, which can be identified by the occurrence of certain characteristic features (see \S~\ref{sec-features ), or other AI-generated media.}
elements.sent-2411-041: I maintain a repository of examples known as Academ-AI (\url{https://www.academ-ai.info/ ).}
elements.sent-2411-042: The purpose of this article is to report the development of this repository and characterize the first 500 examples collected.
elements.sent-2411-043: I will also discuss how journals represented in the repository differ from their peers in terms of publication costs and citation metrics.
elements.sent-2411-044: Examples include ``as an AI language model,'' ``certainly, here are,'' and ``don't have access to real-time.'' The Retraction Watch list of papers with evidence of ChatGPT writing was also used to supplement these searches \autocite{retractionwatch2024 .}
elements.sent-2411-045: Each result was manually examined and included or excluded according to the following criteria.
elements.sent-2411-046: Publication as an academic journal article or conference paper.
elements.sent-2411-048: Phrasing characteristic of AI-generated text or.
elements.sent-2411-049: Statement from the publication or authors that AI was used without declaration.
elements.sent-2411-050: Suspicious phrasing justified by context on manual examination.
elements.sent-2411-051: Any medium other than journal articles and conference papers, such as books, preprints, and technical reports.
elements.sent-2411-052: Publication too early to contain LLM-generated text, as determined by: \begin{enumerate.}
elements.sent-2411-054: Indexing in a reputable database, such as PubMed, Web of Science, or Scopus.
elements.sent-2411-055: However, since unscrupulous publishers could falsify metadata, dates of publication were relied upon only when confirmed by a reputable independent indexing service (criterion \ref{enum-database ).}
elements.sent-2411-056: For each article or paper included, sections of the text containing suspect phrasing were extracted and stored in Markdown files.
elements.sent-2411-057: Citations were collected using Zotero version 6.0.37 \autocite{takats2024 .}
elements.sent-2411-058: Metadata not captured automatically by Zotero were entered manually.
elements.sent-2411-059: The ISSN Portal and the websites of individual publications were used to supplement metadata collection \autocite{issn2024 .}
elements.sent-2411-060: International Standard Serial Numbers (ISSNs) of journals were validated using the ISSN Portal.
elements.sent-2411-061: Those that yielded no results were tested using the ISSN checksum \autocite{abdalla2015 .}
elements.sent-2411-062: Bibliographic data were exported to YAML for analysis using Better BibTeX~version 6.7.210 \autocite{heyns2024 .. \figbyyear.}
elements.sent-2411-063: Information on article processing charges (APCs) was drawn from the website of each journal.
elements.sent-2411-064: APC data were also drawn from the \textcite{doaj to facilitate comparison between DOAJ-indexed journals that were and were not represented in the Academ-AI dataset; where journal websites conflicted with DOAJ-reported APCs, the journal website figure was used, and the discrepancy was reported to the DOAJ.}
elements.sent-2411-065: Foreign exchange rates were drawn from the Open Exchange Rates application programming interface version 0.7, queried October 30, 2024 \autocite{oxr2024 .}
elements.sent-2411-066: Citation metrics were drawn from the Scimago Journal Rank (SJR) database \autocite{sjr .}
elements.sent-2411-067: A natural language processing analysis was conducted using quanteda version 4.0.2 \autocite{benoit2018 .}
elements.sent-2411-068: Text excerpts from the Academ-AI database were tokenized.
elements.sent-2411-069: A dictionary of keywords associated with each of eight textual features of interest was compiled and used to construct a document feature matrix (Table~\ref{tbl-dfm ).}
elements.sent-2411-070: In the case of referral to other sources, the feature was expressed using so many different phrasings that a single set of keywords with sufficient sensitivity and specificity became inefficient.
elements.sent-2411-071: Instead, an excessively sensitive set was used, and results were manually screened.\footnote{For instance, an LLM will sometimes use the words ``recent'' and ``latest'' to refer to information dated shortly prior to its last update, purporting to paraphrase this information; on other occasions, the same words will refer to information dated after that update, to which the user is encouraged to direct their attention since the LLM cannot access it (see \S~\ref{sec-features ).}}
elements.sent-2411-072: Differentiation between the two usages necessitated manual examination of all instances of ``recent'' or ``latest'' in context.
elements.sent-2411-073: With the document feature matrix constructed, documents (articles and papers) were dichotomized as having or lacking each textual feature.
elements.sent-2411-074: Documents were categorized by type (journal article or conference paper), alleged year of publication, and publisher.
elements.sent-2411-075: Journal articles were also categorized according to whether they appeared in a journal with APCs (yes, no, or unknown).
elements.sent-2411-076: APCs were converted to US dollars and summarized, overall and by publisher.
elements.sent-2411-077: To give appropriate weight to containers (journals and conferences) that published multiple documents (articles and papers) in the dataset, documents, rather than containers, were treated as single observations even where reporting and comparing container-level variables, such as publisher or APC.
elements.sent-2411-078: The number of DOAJ-indexed journals that had APCs, stratified by whether or not a given journal was represented in the Academ-AI dataset, was calculated.
elements.sent-2411-079: The same two groups, excluding journals that did not charge APCs, were compared in terms of APC value in US dollars.
elements.sent-2411-080: Additionally, the citation metrics of SJR-indexed journals represented in the Academ-AI dataset were compared with those of all other journals in the SJR database.
elements.sent-2411-081: For the comparisons with the DOAJ and SJR databases, containers (journals) were treated as single observations.
elements.sent-2411-082: Categorical variables were reported as frequencies and percentages and compared using $\chi^2$ tests.
elements.sent-2411-083: Continuous variables were reported as medians and interquartile ranges (IQR) and compared using Wilcoxon rank-sum tests.
elements.sent-2411-084: Tokenization, currency conversion, and analysis were performed using R version 4.4.0 \autocite{chambers2024 .}
elements.sent-2411-085: Years of publication according to the publishers are depicted in Figure~\ref{fig-byyear ; 93.2\% had publication dates in 2022 or later, leaving 6.8\% ostensibly published long before the public release of ChatGPT (November 2022).}
elements.sent-2411-086: Articles were published in 345 different journals, the overwhelming majority of which (86\%) published only one of the articles (Figure~\ref{fig-items-per-container ).}
elements.sent-2411-087: The journals most highly represented in the dataset were the International Journal of Open Publication and Exploration (18 articles) and the International Research Journal of Modernization in Engineering Technology and Science (11 articles).
elements.sent-2411-088: Papers were presented at 45 different conferences, with 91\% of conferences including only one of the papers (Figure~\ref{fig-items-per-container ).}
elements.sent-2411-089: The conference most highly represented in the dataset was the International Conference on Electronics, Communication and Aerospace Technology (4 papers).
elements.sent-2411-090: Only 13.1\% of journal articles were published in journals produced by a major academic publisher, such as Elsevier (5.1\%), Springer (2.2\%), or MDPI (1.1\%); see Figure~\ref{fig-items-per-publisher .}
elements.sent-2411-091: By contrast, the overwhelming majority of conference papers (88.2\%) were published by major academic publishers, chiefly the IEEE (80.4\%).
elements.sent-2411-092: Overall, major publishers produced 20.8\% of the documents in the dataset.
elements.sent-2411-093: All major publishers represented in the dataset require declaration of AI usage in their editorial policies apart from Wolters Kluwer.\footnote{The IOP uses the word ``should'' in their policy but have stated that failure to declare constitutes a ``violation'' \autocite{tarla2023e .}}
elements.sent-2411-094: Only two Wolters Kluwer publications---Medicine and the Annals of Medicine \& Surgery---appear in the dataset, and both require AI declaration in their guidelines for authors, each using verbiage borrowed from the COPE policy \autocite{ams2024,medicine2024 .}
elements.sent-2411-095: At least 295 articles (65.7\%) were published in a journal with some form of publication charge; 21 of these were in journals that mentioned having such a charge on their websites but did not state a specific figure.
elements.sent-2411-096: Among the remaining 274, the median charge was US \$150 (IQR 50--1,295).
elements.sent-2411-097: Charges stratified by publisher are depicted in Figure~\ref{fig-apc-publisher .}
elements.sent-2411-098: Wiley had the highest median APC; major publishers had much higher APCs than other publishers (median [IQR] \$3,039 [2,191--3,530] vs. \$80 [34--300]; $P<0.001$).. \figapcpublisher \figviolin.
elements.sent-2411-099: Thirty-five articles (7.8\%) were published in 33 journals indexed in the DOAJ.\footnote{The Saudi Journal of Biological Sciences, Journal of Human Sport and Exercise, International Journal of Molecular Sciences, and Advances in Mathematical Finance and Applications, though since removed from the DOAJ, were indexed when these articles were published and are included in the analysis.}
elements.sent-2411-100: Of these, 28 journals (84.8\%) had publication charges, a significantly greater proportion than among the remainder of the journals in the database (34.3\%; $P<0.001$).
elements.sent-2411-101: The APCs of journals represented in the Academ-AI dataset (median \$1,982; IQR 194--2,994) were much higher than the rest of the DOAJ (median \$842; IQR 100--2,070; $P=0.039$); see Figure~\ref{fig-violin .}
elements.sent-2411-102: Seventy journals in the SJR database published 82 articles (18.3\%) in the Academ-AI dataset.
elements.sent-2411-103: Compared to other journals in the database, journals represented on Academ-AI had significantly higher median SJR scores, h-indices, publication outputs, citations, and citation rates; see Table~\ref{tbl-sjr-comp and Figure~\ref{fig-sjr-violin .. \tblsjrcomp \figsjrviolin.}}
elements.sent-2411-104: Use of the first-person singular is typical of LLM chatbots but not of multi-author journal articles; it therefore functions as a tell-tale sign of AI involvement.
elements.sent-2411-105: The majority of excerpts in the Academ-AI dataset (55.2\%) featured use of the first-person singular; see Figure~\ref{fig-nlp .}
elements.sent-2411-106: Even in the excerpts from single-author articles, the context made clear on manual examination that the first-person singular pronoun referred to the chatbot, not to the author of the article.
elements.sent-2411-107: LLM chatbots are trained to warn users of their own limitations, such as the knowledge cutoff in their training data.
elements.sent-2411-108: Lack of data after this cutoff date can affect the accuracy of a chatbot's outputs \autocite{openai2024 ; hence, chatbots frequently inform users of their cutoff date or ``last knowledge update'' as in the following example [emphasis added]:.}
elements.sent-2411-109: Using PCSK9 inhibition as a therapeutic approach for COVID-19 presents several challenges and potential limitations.
elements.sent-2411-110: As of my last knowledge update in September 2023, clinical data supporting its use were limited.. \signed{\autocite{arsh2024.}}
elements.sent-2411-111: The purpose of this disclaimer is to account for potential developments since September 2023, specifically more clinical data supporting the use of PCSK9 inhibition as a COVID-19 therapy.
elements.sent-2411-112: Despite the issuance of an erratum \autocite{arsh2024e , the AI-generated text has not been removed from the published article.}
elements.sent-2411-113: Almost half of the articles in the Academ-AI dataset (49.0\%) made some reference to their knowledge cutoff.
elements.sent-2411-114: A common example of this verbiage is a response opening with ``Certainly, here is\ldots{ '' or ``Certainly, here are\ldots{ '', as in the following example [emphasis added]:.}}
elements.sent-2411-115: Certainly, here is a possible introduction for your topic:Lithiummetal {[ sic{] batteries are promising candidates for high-energy-density rechargeable batteries due to their low electrode potentials and high theoretical capacities.. \signed{\autocite{zhang2024.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-116: In this instance, it appears that the chabot was prompted to provide an introduction for the article, and the authors copied the chatbot's helpful preamble along with the generated introduction.
elements.sent-2411-118: The same verbiage appeared in almost a third of the examples in the Academ-AI dataset (31.8\%).
elements.sent-2411-119: In keeping with their caution concerning their own knowledge cutoffs, chatbots are wont to refer users to other sources of information when relevant data in their training sets are sparse or when the subject matter is highly technical.
elements.sent-2411-120: Three separate examples of this behavior were observed in an article in Medicine [emphasis added]: \newcommand\ellipsis{{ .. .}}
elements.sent-2411-121: It's important to note that research in this field is ongoing, and newer studies may have been published since my last update.
elements.sent-2411-122: It's advisable to search for more recent literature to stay current on advancements in our understanding of CD4/CD8 ratios and their implications in various health conditions.
elements.sent-2411-123: For the most current information, it's recommended to consult recent scientific literature or speak with healthcare professionals specializing in HIV care.
elements.sent-2411-124: Just over a fifth of the examples in the Academ-AI dataset (20.6\%) included an encouragement to the user to seek information from more recent or authoritative sources.
elements.sent-2411-125: In addition to referring to themselves using the first-person singular, chatbots also frequently address the user in the second person.
elements.sent-2411-127: If you have additional details or context about SMO or CSMO, please provide them so that I can offer more specific insights.. \signed{\autocite{sumit2024.}}
elements.sent-2411-128: Again, the verbiage is reminiscent of helpful customer service personnel.
elements.sent-2411-129: About one in seven examples (13.8\%) in the dataset involved use of the second person to directly address the user.
elements.sent-2411-130: In early releases of ChatGPT, users could press a button labeled ``Regenerate response,'' instructing the chatbot to make another attempt to answer their prompt.
elements.sent-2411-131: In 58 cases, comprising 11.6\% of the dataset, authors seem to have accidentally copied the button label along with the chatbot's output and pasted both into their manuscripts.
elements.sent-2411-133: However, they neglected to remove the phrase ``regenerate response'' in their haste.. \signed{\autocite{tsai2023e.}}
elements.sent-2411-134: In one notable example, the appearance of the phrase in the reference list led to a reexamination of the entire bibliography, in turn revealing that 18 of the 76 references appear not to exist; rather, they were likely confabulated by the chatbot \autocite{shoukat2024e .}
elements.sent-2411-135: When warning users of their various limitations, chatbots will sometimes identify themselves explicitly as language models, for example [emphasis added]:.
elements.sent-2411-136: In summary, the management of bilateral iatrogenic I'm very sorry, but I don't have access to real-time information or patient-specific data, as I am an AI language model.\footnote{For screenshots of original, see \textcite{cabanac-bader2024 .. \signed{\autocite{bader2024.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-137: This was a comparatively uncommon marker of AI-generated text, appearing on only 8.6\% of examples in the Academ-AI dataset.
elements.sent-2411-138: Chatbots will occasionally warn users that they lack access to data relevant to the prompt, such as ``real-time information or patient-specific data'' as in the example by \textcite{bader2024 ; see \S~\ref{self-identification-as-a-language-model .}}
elements.sent-2411-139: In the Academ-AI dataset, 8.6\% of the examples included such a warning.
elements.sent-2411-140: In one notable example, Frontiers in Cell and Developmental Biology published an article containing AI-generated figures, most infamously a cross-section of anatomically impossible rat genitalia.
elements.sent-2411-141: The article in question was retracted a mere three days after its publication \autocite{guo2024e .}
elements.sent-2411-142: Fifteen articles with suspected AI-generated content in as many journals have been corrected post publication, including six full retractions \autocite{bader2024e,guo2024e,li2023e,shoukat2024e,tarla2023e,zhang2024e , five corrections \autocite{arsh2024e,ezzameli2023e,kanjarla2023e,tsai2023e,yang2023e , and five ``stealth'' corrections: that is, articles corrected without a formal statement \autocite{aquarius2024a .\footnote{See Appendix \ref{app-stealth for details.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-143: In total, therefore, 3.0\% of examples in the dataset were corrected, with 2.2\% being formally corrected and 1.0\% stealth-corrected.
elements.sent-2411-144: Formal corrections occurred a median 104 days (IQR 48--151) after publication; see Figure~\ref{fig-correction .}
elements.sent-2411-145: Journals were faster to issue full retractions (median 50 {[ IQR 38--92{] days) than corrections (median 120 {[ IQR 104--182{] days), but this difference was not statistically significant ($P=0.17$).}}}}
elements.sent-2411-146: One stated that the use of generative AI, while a breach of journal policy, was not the reason for retraction \autocite{bader2024e , and one did not mention AI at all, referring only to ``nonstandard phrases,'' which may be an oblique reference to the phrase ``regenerate response'' \autocite{li2023e .}}
elements.sent-2411-147: In two cases, the AI-generated text was removed \autocite{tsai2023e or enclosed in brackets \autocite{yang2023e , but no declaration of AI usage was added to the main article as required by MDPI and Elsevier policy, respectively.}}
elements.sent-2411-148: In \textcite{arsh2024 , the AI-generated text was persists, and the declaration of AI usage appears only in the erratum \autocite{arsh2024e .}}
elements.sent-2411-149: In \textcite{kanjarla2023 , the AI-generated text was not removed, and there is no declaration of AI usage in the main article, despite the following statement appearing in the corrigendum:.}
elements.sent-2411-150: Sage policy requires authors to declare the use of ChatGPT if used for writing assistance.
elements.sent-2411-151: As such, the acknowledgements section has been updated to disclose this assistance.. \signed{\autocite{kanjarla2023e.}}
elements.sent-2411-152: Of the five corrections, only one succeeded in bringing the original article into compliance with the relevant journal or publisher policy on AI declaration \autocite{ezzameli2023e .}
elements.sent-2411-153: Hundreds of examples of undeclared AI-generated content appear to be present in the academic literature; approximately one in five of these evaded detection by a major publisher with an explicit policy against such content.
elements.sent-2411-154: Journals represented in the dataset more commonly have APCs, and their APCs and citation scores are higher than those of their peers in the DOAJ and SJR database, respectively; in other words, the journals committing editorial oversights concerning AI are paradoxically paid more for editorial processing and achieve greater academic impact.
elements.sent-2411-155: An extremely small proportion of cases are corrected following discovery, and still fewer are corrected formally.
elements.sent-2411-156: Four out of five formal corrigenda failed to revise the original article sufficiently to comply with editorial policy.
elements.sent-2411-157: It seems that in a remarkable number of cases, editorial and peer review have failed to uphold publishing standards with regard to artificial intelligence.
elements.sent-2411-158: Part of the problem may be a lack of awareness of the idiosyncratic phrasing used by LLMs.
elements.sent-2411-159: While some features, such as explicit self-identification ``as an AI language model'' are difficult to miss, it is unsurprising that others, such as inappropriate use of conversational phrasing or the first-person singular, do not immediately lead reviewers to think of AI specifically.
elements.sent-2411-160: Phrasing such as ``as of my last knowledge update'' should, however, alert editors and reviewers that something is amiss, and if errors such as these endure the scrutiny of multiple reviewers and editors, what else are they overlooking? The \textcite{shoukat2024 case is particularly illustrative of this problem.}
elements.sent-2411-161: The appearance of the phrase ``regenerate response'' in the reference list of an article does not in itself diminish the scientific quality of that article any more than a slight misspelling or a poorly formatted paragraph break.
elements.sent-2411-162: However, as an indicator of AI involvement, the error led to the discovery that almost a quarter of the reference list was likely confabulated.
elements.sent-2411-163: That 18 of the authors' sources did not exist went unnoticed throughout the peer review, copyedit, and typesetting processes, and had the authors been fractionally more careful when copying the chatbot's output---avoiding copying the button label along with the response---the confabulation may never have come to light.
elements.sent-2411-164: The tendency of LLM chatbots to confabulate references is well documented (see \textcite{moskatel2024 for example), and many citations to non-existent sources may already be present in the published literature.}
elements.sent-2411-165: In the examples described here, the AI-generated text is sufficiently incongruous with the remainder of the article as to be obvious, but LLM outputs vary widely, and identifying them amid human-authored text is not always straightforward or even possible.
elements.sent-2411-166: Neither humans, nor machines, nor a combination of the two can reliably distinguish AI-generated content from human-authored content \autocite{campbell2023 .}
elements.sent-2411-167: Future AI detection systems may be more advanced, but so will future generative AI systems, meaning that detection will likely continue to present a problem.
elements.sent-2411-168: The examples on Academ-AI were predominantly identifiable due to characteristic turns of phrase, which are only reproduced in a manuscript when authors copy text directly from an AI system with little to no scrutiny.
elements.sent-2411-169: Light proofreading would be sufficient to remove such clear AI fingerprints, though not necessarily sufficient to identify deeper AI-induced problems in the content, such as confabulated information.
elements.sent-2411-170: In other words, once characteristic AI phraseology becomes widely known or AI detectors become more reliable, unscrupulous authors may be incentivized to expend moderate additional effort disguising AI-generated text rather than significant additional effort thoroughly validating it; this would mask the symptoms while failing to treat the underlying disease.
elements.sent-2411-171: It is entirely possible that lightly edited AI-generated text is already abundant in the literature, undetectable, but just as, if not more, flawed than its caveat-riddled, conversationally toned, and therefore identifiable original version.
elements.sent-2411-172: While this hypothesis is deeply troubling, it should not deter us from studying and contending with the examples that are observable, any more than the opacity of dark matter and dark energy should deter physicists from the study of observable matter and energy.
elements.sent-2411-173: It is imperative that we identify and address the instances of undeclared AI that we can detect.
elements.sent-2411-174: In such cases, journals and publishers must enforce their own policies via correction or retraction, depending on the severity of the policy violation.
elements.sent-2411-175: To do otherwise sets a precedent of policy violations going unaddressed, suggesting to authors that declaration of AI usage is unnecessary.
elements.sent-2411-176: By contrast, a precedent of enforcing these policies incentivizes declaration among careless and even unscrupulous authors on the off-chance that the use of AI is detectable in their case.
elements.sent-2411-177: I have previously drawn a comparison to conflict of interest \autocite{glynn2024b .}
elements.sent-2411-178: Briefly, scientific research has successfully transitioned in recent decades from a prohibitive model, in which competing interests are disqualifying, to a transparent model, in which competing interests are disclosed and managed \autocite[ pp.~61--63]{iom1991 .}
elements.sent-2411-179: With disclosure becoming commonplace and failure to disclose meeting severe consequences, researchers have every incentive to disclose their conflicts rather than concealing them.
elements.sent-2411-180: We can use the same incentives to encourage transparency with regard to AI usage, but this depends on journals enforcing their own policies.
elements.sent-2411-181: Given the risks posed by the inclusion of AI-generated text in research literature, publishers and journals must establish a precedent of enforcing their policies against undeclared AI usage in the writing process.
elements.sent-2411-182: When policy violations that escape the notice of editors and peer reviewers are identified post publication, formal corrections must be made.
elements.sent-2411-183: The author received no specific funding for this work.
elements.sent-2411-184: The author declares no conflict of interest.
elements.sent-2411-185: Statements affirming that AI systems must not be credited as authors are highlighted in orange.
elements.sent-2411-186: Statements affirming that the use of AI tools in the writing process must be declared are highlighted in blue.
elements.subsubsection-2411-011: International Committee of Medical Journal Editors (ICMJE)
elements.subsubsection-2411-013: Institute of Electrical and Electronics Engineeers (IEEE)
elements.sent-2411-187: When doing so, \hldecl{authors are encouraged to be transparent about their use of any generative AI tools in either the research or the drafting of the manuscript.. \fullcite{iopnd.}}
elements.sent-2411-188: Authors who use AI-assisted technologies as components of their research study or as aids in the writing or presentation of the manuscript \hldecl{should note this in the cover letter and in the acknowledgments section of the manuscript.. \fullcite{science2024.}}
elements.sent-2411-189: Authors who use AI tools in the writing of a manuscript [...] \hldecl{must be transparent in disclosing in the Materials and Methods (or similar section) of the paper how the AI tool was used and which tool was used.. \fullcite{ams2024.}}
elements.sent-2411-190: The phrase ``Regenerate response'' was removed from the article \autocite{cabanac-ezzameli2023 .}
elements.sent-2411-191: The first author confirmed that the phrase was the result of using ChatGPT \autocite{ezzameli-pubpeer2023 .}
elements.sent-2411-192: An acknowledgement to this effect was added to the article, and a formal correction was eventually published \autocite{ezzameli2023e .}
elements.sent-2411-193: The phrase ``Regenerate response'' was removed from the article \autocite{cabanac-mohamed2023 .}
elements.sent-2411-194: The first author confirmed that the phrase was the result of using AI tools \autocite{mohamed-pubpeer2023 .}
elements.sent-2411-195: Since no declaration of AI usage has been added to the article, it is still in violation of Elsevier policy.
elements.sent-2411-196: The phrase ``Regenerate response'' was removed from the article between July 27, 2023, and September 19, 2023 \autocite{cabanac-akhter2023 .}
elements.sent-2411-198: The phrase ``Regenerate response'' was removed from the article \autocite{cabanac-rini2023 .}
elements.sent-2411-199: No formal correction was published, but as of September 27, 2024, the following statement had been added to the new version of the article: \excerpt{This article only used AI for proof-reading, while idea and content are the results of author’s research and thought.}
elements.sent-2411-200: At least one more stealth correction seems to have occurred; as of November 20, 2024, the article contains neither the AI-generated phrase nor the statement concerning AI use.
elements.sent-2411-201: The phrase ``Regenerate response'' was removed from the article.
elements.sent-2411-202: No formal correction was published, but the following statement was added to the new version of the article: \excerpt{This article has been republished with minor changes.}
elements.sent-2411-203: These changes do not impact the academic content of the article.
elements.sent-2411-204: The nature of the correction is not specified, and its impact (or lack thereof) is decided for the reader.
elements.sent-2411-205: Additionally, since there is still no acknowledgement of AI usage in the article, it appears to remain non-compliant with Taylor \& Francis policy.
elements.sent-2411-206: Given the lack of transparency in this approach, I consider it closer to a stealth correction than a formal correction.. \sloppy \end{appendices.}
